game.title: The Opera Game
game.narrative_intro: Paris Opera House, 1858. Paul Morphy, the American prodigy, is at the board with White against two aristocrats sharing the black pieces. They would rather watch the opera — and Morphy is about to make the game very short. You are White.

moves.annotation: Bg5 develops the last minor piece and pins the knight, keeping Black's king stuck in the center. White is clearly better. Be3 is playable but passive; a4, O-O, and g4 are all slower and let Black untangle.

alt_annotations.c1e3: Solid but passive — it lets Black begin to untangle.
alt_annotations.a2a4: Useful one day, but too slow while Black's king sits in the center.
alt_annotations.e1g1: Safe, yet castling here eases the pressure; the initiative matters more.
alt_annotations.g2g4: Loosening and slow — Black gets time to consolidate.

moves.annotation: Nxb5!! rips open the c-file: after Black recaptures, White's pieces pour toward the stranded king. The timid Bxf6, Bxb5, Be2, and Bd5 all let Black off the hook and squander most of the advantage.

alt_annotations.g5f6: Trades off an attacker; the pressure fizzles and White is only slightly better.
alt_annotations.c4b5: Takes the pawn back but releases the bind — most of the edge is gone.
alt_annotations.c4e2: Passive retreat; Black gets time to develop and the edge slips.
alt_annotations.c4d5: Tempting, but after the recapture the attack loses steam.

moves.annotation: O-O-O drops the rook onto the open d-file, where it bears down on Black's pinned knight and queen — castling as a killing blow. Bxf6 lets a defender off the hook; Rd1, a3, and O-O are all much weaker.

alt_annotations.g5f6: Releases the pin too soon; White stays better but the bind loosens.
alt_annotations.a1d1: Bringing the other rook up leaves the king in the center — far less forcing.
alt_annotations.a2a3: A waiting move that wastes the moment and lets Black breathe.
alt_annotations.e1g1: Castling the wrong way misplaces the rook and lets Black's bishop into the attack.

moves.annotation: Rxd7! tears down Black's last defender — after the recapture, the second rook swings in and the d-file collapses. Rd3, Ba4, Rd5, and Kb1 keep an edge but let Black survive much longer.

alt_annotations.d1d3: Lifts the rook but gives Black time to consolidate.
alt_annotations.b5a4: Keeps the bishop, yet it's slow; Black defends and holds on.
alt_annotations.d1d5: Active, but Black can kick the rook and the punch is gone.
alt_annotations.c1b1: Tidying the king is too slow when a forcing finish is available.

moves.annotation: Rd1 brings the last piece into the attack, piling a second rook onto the pinned knight. Nothing else compares: Bxf6, c3, Ba4, and f4 all squander the initiative.

alt_annotations.g5f6: Trading the bishop eases Black's defense and softens the attack.
alt_annotations.c2c3: A quiet pawn move wastes the tempo Black desperately needs.
alt_annotations.b5a4: Retreating the bishop lets Black reorganize.
alt_annotations.f2f4: Premature; it opens lines for Black's queen instead.

moves.annotation: Bxd7+ clears the way with check, forcing Black's reply so the rook can crash through next. Bxf6 is also strong, but Qc3, Rxd7, and Qxe6+ let the win slide toward merely better.

alt_annotations.g5f6: Also crushing — but Bxd7+ is cleaner and more forcing.
alt_annotations.b3c3: Strong, yet it lets Black wriggle free and defend.
alt_annotations.d1d7: Right idea, wrong order — Black grabs the White queen first.
alt_annotations.b3e6: Takes the queen, but after the recapture only a small edge remains.

moves.annotation: Qb8+!! is the famous queen sacrifice — the knight is forced to take, abandoning the d-file, and the rook mates on the back rank. The greedy Qb7 and Qc3 keep a big edge but miss the forced mate; Qb5 and Rd5 are much worse.

alt_annotations.b3b7: Keeps a winning attack, but lets the king flee and misses mate in two.
alt_annotations.b3c3: Strong, yet it allows a longer fight instead of the immediate mate.
alt_annotations.b3b5: Lets most of the advantage slip — Black gets to defend.
alt_annotations.d1d5: Misplaces the rook and lets the win slip away.

moves.annotation: Rd8#!! is the storybook finish — with the knight no longer guarding the back rank, the rook crashes in to deliver mate. Every other move — f4, Bh4, Kb1, Be3 — throws away the whole game.

alt_annotations.f2f4: Ignores the mate in one, and now White is actually lost.
alt_annotations.g5h4: Retreating throws away mate; Black takes over.
alt_annotations.c1b1: A king move when mate was on the board — now White is losing.
alt_annotations.g5e3: Misses the mate entirely and hands Black the game.
